title: Spotify co-CEOs want to avoid one decision about GenAI music - Music Ally
text:
Spotify’s incoming co-CEOs have
given an interview to The Times
in which they outline one AI-music decision they want to leave to listeners. “We do not decide what are the right tools for what is good creativity. That is something consumers decide,” said Gustav Söderström. “What we make sure is that there is no impersonation of likeness, no abuse of the royalty payout system. We are not deciders of what is good creativity. We don’t think we should decide what [users] are allowed to listen to.”
The comments have already drawn criticism from AI-rights campaigner Ed Newton-Rex however. “They’re not just deciding whether users *can* listen to AI music. They’re refusing to make it clear to users that what they’re listening to *is* AI music,”
tweeted Newton-Rex
. “You listen to AI music on Spotify and you have no idea that you are doing so. They don’t tell you it’s AI. Not to mention that it’s not just buried in their catalog for you to find if you want it. It’s being recommended to you in personalized playlists.”
However, there is scope for Spotify’s policy to change. The company said last month that it is
backing a new industry standard for music credits
that would disclose how and where AI was used in tracks. “As content providers begin to submit the metadata to us, over time, we’ll be displaying it across Spotify,” said Spotify’s Sam Duboff at the time. He also hinted at potential future measures “if someone decided not to use them” (the credits) – although that would require Spotify to have tech in place to detect the unlabelled GenAI tracks.
So, we’re not sure if Spotify and Newton-Rex are so far apart in what they think should be done ultimately. It’s the timescale of it that remains unclear.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
More by Stuart Dredge
